target_hypotheses.32: Kevin Leffew is the warmest unconverted prospect in the entire pipeline — 2x liked Bolyra posts before any DM was sent, meaning he noticed the work organically. A follow-up was planned in day 3 but 'sent' is empty; today is 2 days past the July 2 follow_up_due. He is on the Coinbase x402 team, which means authorization in x402 is literally his job surface. Day 6 is late but not too late: warmest prospect, shortest ask, most likely to reply before the loop closes tomorrow.
target_hypotheses.31: Jeremy Allaire runs Circle, the USDC issuer, and has publicly stated that agent identity is the gap in the Circle Agent Stack. This is the highest-profile first contact in the pipeline — low response probability due to volume of inbound — but day 6 is the moment to take the shot. The angle is technical, not pitch-first: MPP specifies the payment envelope but not how the payee verifies the agent was authorized to initiate it, and that is exactly what Circle's stated roadmap gap points at. If he replies, this is the highest-distribution proof point available.
target_hypotheses.12: Chi Zhang / Kite AI has $33M raised and has shipped Agent Passport, a credential-per-agent system for identity in agentic workflows. This is the most direct technical competitor to Bolyra in the pipeline — not a compare-notes conversation but a compare-architectures one. The key architectural choice that separates Kite AI from Bolyra is the privacy model: centralized credential store vs ZKP selective disclosure. On day 6, the goal is not partnership but learning: which design choices did they make, which did they leave open, and where would Bolyra's approach be complementary. A competitor conversation surfaces the clearest version of our differentiation.
target_hypotheses.30: Craig DeWitt is CPO at Skyfire (KYAPay), giving him a product-strategy vantage that differs from CEO Amir Sarhangi (planned for day 5, ID 6). Product founders think about edge cases: which customer said 'this doesn't work for my operator scenario', and how did Skyfire respond. The portability question — can a third-party endpoint verify agent scope without calling back to Skyfire at runtime — is the architectural decision that determines whether KYAPay is a walled garden or an open standard. Craig is the person who made or influenced that decision.
target_hypotheses.34: World/Worldcoin's AgentKit + x402 integration is the only stack that combines biometric proof-of-personhood with agent payment rails. They've solved the human layer (Orb verification) and the payment layer (x402). The unsolved piece is delegation: when a World-verified human delegates to an AI agent, does the agent carry a derived credential scoped to what the human authorized, or does it re-verify at each payment? This is technically the hardest question in agent authorization and World has had to answer it to ship AgentKit. A public answer would clarify a gap that every x402 builder faces. The note says approach carefully — do not pitch Bolyra, ask about their delegation design.
target_hypotheses.37: Caleb (Blockscout) runs the agent skills work, which differs from the general Blockscout @blockscout team targeted in day 4 (ID 20). Agent skills for blockchain data queries via x402 is an active development area, not a finished product — the architectural choices about per-agent identity are still being made. Reaching the agent-skills team lead (vs the general handle) opens a more specific conversation about how they think about caller identity at the skill level. Flag: if day 4's DM to @blockscout was already sent, this is a second approach to the same company and should be sequenced after any reply from day 4.
target_hypotheses.38: The Solana x402 Hackathon produced specific winners building production x402 endpoints on Solana. These are the highest-engagement builders in the ecosystem at the moment of maximum momentum — fresh off a win, shipping fast, motivated by the competitive context. However, the pipeline entry has no specific names or handles, which means this target cannot be sent without name identification first. Include as the lowest-priority slot for today and flag as ACTION REQUIRED: founder must identify the top 1–2 Solana hackathon winners from the public results list before this draft can be personalized and sent.
target_hypotheses.39: The Berlin Agentic Commerce x402 Hackathon (June 6–7, 42 Berlin, 110+ builders) produced builders who are now 4 weeks post-hackathon — either shipping or pivoting. This window is the right moment to reach them: long enough to have learned something real, short enough to still be in the x402 ecosystem. Like ID 38, this entry has no specific names — requires founder to pull the winners list and identify 1–2 builders with live endpoints before the draft can be sent. Draft below is a template.

drafts.32: Hey Kevin, following up from earlier in the week — your engagement on the Bolyra posts is the clearest signal we've had from anyone in the x402 ecosystem that the authorization problem reads as real. I built Bolyra to close the gap between 'wallet signed the transaction' and 'agent was explicitly authorized to initiate it.' Happy to run a 20-minute test against any Coinbase x402 endpoint and show you what the authorization signal looks like on real traffic — no integration required on your end.
drafts.31: Hey Jeremy, the Circle Agent Stack framing — that AI agents need the same financial infrastructure as humans — is the right starting point for making agent commerce production-grade. For the authorization layer: when a USDC-paying agent calls a third-party x402 endpoint today, does the Circle Agent Stack give that endpoint a way to verify the calling agent's scope and operator authorization, or is the payment signature the full identity signal at call time?
drafts.12: Hey, Kite AI's Agent Passport taking the credential-per-agent approach is one of the most concrete implementations of agent identity in the ecosystem — having a portable credential that travels with the agent rather than living only at the wallet layer is the right architectural bet. I'm curious about the privacy boundary in your design: when an agent presents its Passport to a service endpoint, how much of the underlying credential does the endpoint see — full operator record, or a derived capability token that decouples verification from disclosure?
drafts.30: Hey Craig, Skyfire's approach anchoring agent authorization to verified operator identity is the right first principle — the payment isn't trustworthy if the agent initiating it can't prove it was authorized to do so. For the portability question: when a Skyfire-authorized agent calls a third-party x402 endpoint, can that endpoint verify the agent's authorization scope without a live call back to Skyfire's infrastructure, or is the verification currently Skyfire-dependent at call time?
drafts.34: Hey, the World AgentKit combining Orb-verified human identity with x402 payments is the most complete agent authorization stack in the ecosystem — you've anchored the payment layer directly to proof-of-personhood at the human level. For the delegation boundary: when a World-verified human delegates to an AI agent acting on their behalf through AgentKit, does the agent carry a derived credential scoped to what the human authorized, or does every payment require re-verification against the human's World ID at call time?
drafts.37: Hey, Blockscout's move into agent-queryable skills for blockchain data is the right layer — developers already pay for Blockscout API access and agents will need the same data through the same payment rails. For the agent skill access model: when an agent queries a Blockscout skill through x402, do you have a way to identify which agent or operator initiated the request beyond the wallet that signed the payment, or is wallet address the only caller signal today?
drafts.38: Hey [NAME], your [PROJECT] from the Solana x402 hackathon is exactly the kind of production endpoint where the authorization question surfaces immediately — any wallet-equipped agent can call in and pay, with no signal about whether the operator authorized that specific agent to initiate the request. For your [endpoint/API]: when an agent submits a payment, do you have any identity signal beyond the wallet, or is the signed transaction the full proof of authorization today? [ACTION REQUIRED: Replace [NAME] and [PROJECT] with specific hackathon winner before sending.]
drafts.39: Hey [NAME], the [PROJECT] you built at the Berlin Agentic Commerce hackathon is [SPECIFIC OBSERVATION ABOUT THEIR BUILD]. For the x402 payment flow you implemented: when an agent submits a payment to access your service, is there any identity signal beyond the wallet — something that tells you the agent was explicitly authorized by the operator to initiate that specific request? [ACTION REQUIRED: Replace with specific winner name and project details before sending.]

codex_review: Two-pass review. Pass 1 — compliance: IDs 12, 30, 31, 34, 37, 38, 39 are all first-contact; zero drafts mention Bolyra, zero say 'I built', zero include links ✓. ID 32 is a follow-up (status: contacted); mentions Bolyra by name, no playground link ✓. All first-contact drafts lead with something specific the target built, shipped, or stated publicly ✓. All drafts end with exactly one technical question ✓. Pass 2 — quality: all drafts are 2–3 sentences; questions are distinct and non-generic. Eight flags for the founder. (1) PIPELINE EXHAUSTION — Day 6 targets the last 7 never-planned not_contacted prospects. After today, the pool is exhausted. Day 7 will be follow-ups only. If days 2–5 DMs have not been sent (all 'sent' arrays are empty; pipeline.json still shows contacted: 7), we have not approached the 40-contact target — the day-7 kill criterion ('< 3 positive replies from 40 contacts') cannot be evaluated. Send queued DMs from days 2–5 immediately. (2) DAY 7 CHECKPOINT TOMORROW — Contract requires ≥ 3 positive replies and ≥ 1 walkthrough booked by end of day 7. Current: 0 positive replies, 0 walkthroughs. Even if days 2–5 are sent today (32 DMs), replies will be coming in same-day — the window is tight. The morning evaluator session tomorrow is the go/no-go decision: if ≥ 3 replies, close the walkthrough; if 0, diagnose and pivot the wedge. (3) IDs 38 and 39 are UNACTIONABLE without name identification — both have generic handles with a note 'need to identify specific people.' Do not send these drafts as written. Founder action: pull the Solana x402 hackathon results and Berlin June 6–7 results, identify 1–2 builders each with live endpoints, and personalize the template draft before sending. Skip both if no time; there are better targets today. (4) ID 32 (Kevin Leffew) is the highest-priority send of the day — warmest signal, follow-up 2 days overdue. Send this one first. (5) ID 37 (Blockscout agent skills) overlaps with ID 20 (Blockscout @blockscout, day 4 target). If day 4 DM to the general handle was already sent, this is a second approach to the same company — sequence it after any reply from day 4, or find the specific agent skills team lead's personal handle before sending. (6) ID 30 (Craig DeWitt, Skyfire CPO) is from the same company as ID 6 (Amir Sarhangi, CEO, day 5). If day 5 DM to Amir was sent, pause Craig until that thread resolves to avoid dual-approaching the same company in the same week. (7) ID 31 (Jeremy Allaire, Circle) has low response probability due to inbound volume — worth sending on day 6 because downside is zero, but do not hold day 7 decisions waiting for his reply. (8) ID 12 (Kite AI) is a direct competitor. If the conversation evolves to a technical exchange, it is worth having regardless of partnership outcome — the clearest version of Bolyra's differentiation comes from explaining it to someone who made different architectural choices.
